source.name: SSA Canonical Catalog
notes: Low-and-slow smoked whole packer brisket with simple salt + pepper rub.
Designed for SSA planners/adapters: clear equipment, methods, timers, and doneness guidance.

yield.notes: Servings vary widely by brisket size and slicing thickness (plan ~1/2 lb cooked per adult, more for hearty eaters).

ingredients.label: Whole packer beef brisket (12–16 lb), preferably Choice+
ingredients.notes: Includes both point + flat. Smaller briskets cook faster but can be less forgiving.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt
ingredients.notes: For a classic Texas rub, use equal parts salt and pepper by volume.

ingredients.label: Garlic powder (optional)
ingredients.notes: Optional for a slightly more aromatic rub; omit for strict salt/pepper.

ingredients.label: Mustard powder (optional)
ingredients.notes: Optional; adds a subtle tang in the bark.

ingredients.label: Yellow mustard (binder, optional)
ingredients.notes: Very thin layer to help rub adhere; flavor largely cooks off.

ingredients.label: Smoking wood (oak preferred; pecan/hickory optional)
ingredients.notes: Amount depends on smoker type. Favor oak/pecan for a balanced smoke; use mesquite sparingly.

ingredients.label: Apple cider vinegar (for spritz, optional)
ingredients.notes: Mix 1:1 with water in a spray bottle for spritzing bark after it sets.

ingredients.label: Water (for spritz, optional)
ingredients.notes: For spritzing. Helps maintain surface moisture without washing off rub once bark is set.

steps.title: Trim brisket for even rendering
steps.body: Trim the fat cap to about 1/4 inch thickness. Remove hard, waxy fat and any thin/loose edges that will burn. Shape the brisket so airflow is smooth and the flat is protected (rounded corners help prevent drying). Save trimmings for tallow if desired.

steps.title: Season with Texas-style rub
steps.body: Mix kosher salt and coarse black pepper (add garlic powder/mustard powder if using). Optionally apply a very thin mustard binder. Season brisket evenly on all sides, pressing rub to adhere. Let sit 15–30 minutes while you prepare the smoker (or refrigerate uncovered 2–12 hours for a drier surface and better bark).

steps.title: Preheat smoker and stabilize temperature
steps.body: Preheat smoker to 225–250°F (107–121°C). Add smoking wood according to your smoker type. Aim for clean smoke (thin blue), not billowing white smoke. Place a water pan if your smoker uses one to help temperature stability.

steps.title: Start smoking (fat cap orientation as needed)
steps.body: Place brisket on smoker. Orientation depends on heat source: fat cap toward the hotter side to protect the flat. Insert a probe thermometer into the thickest part of the flat (center, not touching fat pockets). Close the lid and maintain 225–250°F.

steps.title: Optional spritz after bark sets
steps.body: After 2–4 hours, when the surface looks dry and the rub has set into a bark (not wet/patchy), you may spritz lightly every 45–60 minutes with a 50/50 mix of water and apple cider vinegar. Do not spritz early or heavily, which can wash off rub and delay bark formation.

steps.title: Wrap at the stall (Texas crutch)
steps.body: When bark is a deep mahogany and internal temp in the flat is roughly 165–175°F (74–79°C) and the brisket enters the stall (temp rise slows), wrap tightly in butcher paper (preferred) or foil. Return to smoker with the seam side down.

steps.title: Finish cooking until probe-tender
steps.body: Continue cooking at 250°F until the brisket is probe-tender in multiple spots, especially the flat—meaning a probe slides in with little resistance (like soft butter). This typically occurs around 195–205°F (90–96°C), but tenderness is the true finish line.

steps.title: Rest (critical for juiciness and slicing)
steps.body: Rest the wrapped brisket at least 1 hour. For best results, hold 2–4 hours in a warm cooler (towel-wrapped) or a low oven (150–170°F / 65–77°C) if available. Resting allows juices to redistribute and collagen to relax for tenderness.

steps.title: Slice and serve
steps.body: Unwrap and separate point and flat if desired. Slice the flat across the grain into pencil-thick slices. The point can be sliced thicker or cubed for burnt ends. Serve with pickles/onions, optional sauce, and classic sides like slaw, beans, or potato salad.

steps.title: Storage and reheating notes
steps.body: Cool leftovers quickly. Refrigerate sliced brisket with some juices/tallow, tightly covered, up to 4 days. Freeze portions up to 3 months. Reheat gently (covered) with a splash of broth/juices to prevent drying—low oven or sous-vide-style warm water bath works well.

leftovers.notes: Plan brisket day as a batch cook; schedule leftover uses over the next 2–3 days.

notes: If no smoker: use oven + liquid smoke cautiously, or slow-braise (not true BBQ but can be tasty).
If using a pellet smoker: stabilize temperature and avoid frequent lid opening.
Finish line is probe-tender, not a specific temperature.
Rest/hold is essential for tenderness and slice quality.
